comments.text: This conjecture has been disproved by B. S. Ho in [Ho26]. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Updated, but I don't think this disproves the original conjecture, which likely had in mind the regime where $d$ is fixed and $n\to \infty$.

For convenient reference, Erdős wrote, "Let $\mathscr C$ be a set of $n$ points in Euclidean space among which all distances differ by at least $1$. A conjecture independent of dimension is that $\operatorname{diam}\mathscr C\ge(1+o(1))n^2$. Clearly $\operatorname{diam}\mathscr C$ is always at least $\binom n2$." My initial interpretation was the one with dimension being allowed to vary with $n$, because of the phrase "independent of dimension." But since Erdős wrote "The conjecture is settled only for $\mathscr C\subset\mathbb R$ (not even for $\mathbb R^2$)", it appears reasonable that he had the fixed-dimension version in mind.

Yes, that phrase is confusing, but the overall context (and generally the type of questions in this area he usually asked) suggests the fixed dimension interpretation, as you say in the final sentence. I'd guess that maybe by 'independent of dimension' he meant that the 'main order term' should be always $n^2$, whose form is independent of dimension, rather than the error term being independent of dimension. But your construction is a nice one, and a useful contribution in this direction!
